cataloging_subject.label: The Chronicle of John Hardyng (second version)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is John Hardyng's unfinished Yorkist, or second, version of his Middle English verse Chronicle, revised after the first-version presentation of 1457 and continued for Edward IV. It includes Hardyng's proem, the verse historical narrative, and the final unnumbered Scotland itinerary represented in CME00023. It excludes Hardyng's distinct first version, Richard Grafton's prose continuation, Henry Ellis's nineteenth-century biographical preface and apparatus as independent modern authorship, the physical manuscripts and printed books, and the U-M and repository encodings as digital layers.

catalog_summary.title: The Chronicle of John Hardyng
catalog_summary.author: John Hardyng
catalog_summary.date: Revised after 1457 and still in progress in or after September 1464; unfinished
catalog_summary.region: England; exact place or places of second-version revision not securely established

assertion.notes: The cataloging-subject label adds '(second version)' to distinguish the intellectual unit; that qualifier is not treated as part of the conventional work title.
Ellis's volume title also advertises Grafton's continuation; the title is retained for discovery without expanding the cataloged work boundary to that continuation.

agents.name: John Hardyng
notes: Author of both versions of the Chronicle. The second version revised the work for Richard, duke of York, and was continued for Edward IV before remaining unfinished.

agents.name: Richard Grafton
notes: Printer-editor of the two 1543 editions and author of the prose continuation excluded from the cataloged Hardyng work.

agents.name: Henry Ellis
notes: Editor of the 1812 immediate source edition represented by CME00023; not an author of the medieval work.

agents.name: Philip Bliss
notes: Credited by Ellis with principal collations from Bodleian MS Arch. Selden. B. 10.

scope.description: The distinct second-version revision and its Edwardian continuation

assertion.notes: The second version developed through more than one political and textual stage; a single completion event is not asserted.

scope.description: The 1543 printed source layer through which Hardyng's second version reached Ellis; not medieval authorship

notes: Grafton's separate prose continuation is excluded from the work unit even though it follows Hardyng in both 1543 prints and Ellis's volume.

scope.description: The reset 1812 representation used by CME00023, not the composition of the medieval work
The 1812 source text and apparatus, using two 1543 prints and manuscript readings
Principal readings supplied to Ellis from Bodleian MS Arch. Selden. B. 10

date_statements.display: Revised after the first-version presentation of 1457 and still in progress in or after September 1464; left unfinished

assertion.notes: Peverley describes revision first for Richard, duke of York, then continuation for Edward IV after December 1460. The METS introduction notes that the last datable entry cannot predate September 1464. No single completion year is supplied because the work remained unfinished.

date_statements.display: First printed by Richard Grafton at London in January 1543, in two distinct impressions or editions

assertion.notes: STC 12766.7 is the fuller first printing; STC 12767 is a shorter revised printing from the same month.

date_statements.display: Legendary origins of Britain through the opening years of Edward IV's reign and a concluding itinerary for campaigning in Scotland

places.label: England
notes: Used for the work's broad cultural and political origin; it does not imply a securely localized place of composition or a witness-specific dialect.

assertion.notes: The work participates in English chronicle and Yorkist political culture; the exact location or sequence of locations in which Hardyng revised it remains unresolved.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Latin phrases and short passages embedded within the primarily Middle English work

notes: Modern English in Ellis's apparatus and Grafton's prefatory prose belongs to edition or paratext layers rather than the cataloged medieval work's primary language statement.

form_statements.description: A long Middle English historical chronicle predominantly in seven-line stanzas, represented by 12,535 direct work-text L elements in 1,807 LG elements across the proem and poem divisions.

assertion.notes: Prose chapter arguments, editorial notes, headings, and prefatory prose in the source edition do not make the cataloged Hardyng work a mixed prose-and-verse composition.
The work constructs dynastic and political history for particular fifteenth-century audiences rather than serving as a neutral record of events.

subject_statements.label: Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy
Anglo-Scottish relations, claims, geography, and military planning

assertion.notes: The statement describes the work's political and military subject matter; it does not endorse its territorial claims or proposed violence.

summaries.value: A long Middle English verse history that begins with Albion and Brutus, traces British and English rulers through legendary, Roman, Anglo-Saxon, Danish, Norman, Plantagenet, and Lancastrian pasts, and continues into the opening of Edward IV's reign. Hardyng reshaped the Chronicle's genealogies, political counsel, and claims about England and Scotland for Yorkist and then Edwardian patrons. The unfinished second version closes in the represented text with an itinerary and program for moving armies through Scotland.

The intrinsic work text represented in CME00023 comprises a 287-line proem, 246 numbered XML chapter containers totaling 12,024 verse lines, and a final unnumbered 224-line Scotland itinerary. The inherited printed and XML chapter numerals repeat, skip, or disagree in places, so the 247 DIV2 containers are reported as encoding structures rather than promoted to a canonical chapter count.

CME00023 also preserves Ellis's 1812 title leaf and Grafton's 1543 title, dedication, and preface, but it omits Ellis's xxi-page preface, Grafton's prose continuation on printed pages 431–607, and the index. Those source-edition layers are documented in lineage and do not expand the cataloged intellectual unit beyond Hardyng's second-version verse work.

extent.description: Encoding-specific intrinsic-work metrics: the BODY has one proem DIV1 and one poem DIV1 containing 247 DIV2 containers, for 248 modeled section containers. Excluding 300 L elements nested inside editorial NOTE elements leaves 12,535 direct verse lines in 1,807 LG elements: 287 lines in 41 proem groups and 12,248 lines in 1,766 poem groups. Of the poem total, 246 numbered containers hold 12,024 lines in 1,734 groups and the final unnumbered Scotland itinerary holds 224 lines in 32 groups. These counts do not establish canonical authorial lineation or chapter numbering.

content_parts.label: Proem
content_parts.summary: Hardyng introduces his age, purpose, historical undertaking, authorities, and intention to provide instruction and political counsel.

content_parts.label: Chronicle in numbered XML containers
content_parts.summary: The main historical sequence from Albion and Brutus through Hardyng's account of Britain's and England's rulers, genealogies, wars, and claims, ending with the maker's excusation and counsel to Edward IV.
notes: There are 246 DIV2 containers with an N attribute, but inherited heading numerals include repetitions, gaps, and disagreements; item_count is therefore an encoding count, not a canonical count of medieval chapters.

content_parts.label: Final unnumbered Scotland itinerary
content_parts.summary: A route-and-distance account for moving armies by land and water through major Scottish places, ending with proposed coordinated campaigning and a renewed address to the patron.
notes: Ellis's footnote says this whole section from p. 422 is omitted in Harley MS 661; the section's reading-level dependence on other sources remains an editorial question.

related_works.title: The Chronicle of John Hardyng (first version)
notes: The first version was presented to Henry VI in 1457 and is textually and politically distinct. Simpson and Peverley's 2015 METS volume edits this first version, not CME00023's second version.

related_works.title: Richard Grafton's prose continuation of Hardyng's Chronicle
notes: Grafton's continuation follows Hardyng in the 1543 prints and occupies printed pp. 431–607 in Ellis's 1812 volume, but it is not encoded in CME00023 and is not part of Hardyng's medieval verse work.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00023.xml.
evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD and IDG; EEBO/TEXT/FRONT and BODY; proem and poem divisions
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for the CME identifier, Hardyng and Ellis names, 1812 bibliographic source, Middle English and Latin declaration, encoded title and prefatory layers, proem, poem, final Scotland itinerary, printed-page markers, notes, and revision history.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of the pinned CME00023 XML artifact.
evidence.locator: Counts and nesting of DIV1, DIV2, LG, L, NOTE, PB, MILESTONE, HEAD, ARGUMENT, LIST, and P
evidence.summary: Inspection found 13,206 L and 1,931 LG elements overall. Excluding 300 lines nested in editorial notes leaves 12,535 intrinsic BODY verse lines in 1,807 groups: proem 287/41 and poem 12,248/1,766. The poem has 246 numbered DIV2 containers totaling 12,024/1,734 and one unnumbered final container totaling 224/32. Numbering inconsistencies make these encoding metrics rather than a canonical chapter count.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME00023, The Chronicle of Iohn Hardyng.
evidence.summary: Official collection evidence for the stable CME identity, item title, public encoded text, publication statement, and current rights label.

evidence.citation: John Hardyng, The Chronicle of Iohn Hardyng, ed. Henry Ellis (London, 1812), complete Harvard–Google scan supplied by Internet Archive.
evidence.locator: Title; Ellis preface pp. xiv–xxi; Hardyng printed pp. 15–429; continuation pp. 431–607
evidence.summary: Immediate-source evidence for the represented titles and paratext, Hardyng work boundaries, printed pagination, Ellis's preferred Grafton print, duplicate-print variants, Harley 661 collation, Arch. Selden. B. 10 collations, and bracket conventions.

evidence.citation: Sarah L. Peverley, John Hardyng (University of Liverpool, deposited 2021).
evidence.locator: Two-version chronology, second-version circulation and survival, 1543 print history, Ellis 1812, and modern editorial state
evidence.summary: Current scholarly evidence for Hardyng's authorship, the first and second versions, 1457 presentation, Yorkist and Edwardian revision stages, unfinished status, manuscript and print transmission, Grafton's two 1543 prints, and the limitations of Ellis's complete second-version edition.

evidence.citation: James Simpson and Sarah Peverley, eds., John Hardyng's Chronicle, volume I, Middle English Texts Series, online introduction.
evidence.locator: Introduction sections distinguishing the two versions and discussing second-version chronology and transmission
evidence.summary: Scholarly evidence that the second version is unfinished, exceeds 12,400 lines, contains material not predating September 1464, and survives in twelve manuscripts plus fragments and two prints; the linked edition itself represents the separate first version.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog record 399199, The chronicle of Ihon Hardyng in metre, STC 12766.7, ESTC S103772.
evidence.summary: Official catalog evidence for the fuller first 1543 Grafton print's title, London imprint, January date, extent, and bibliographic identifiers.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts, Harley MS 661, record 040-002046490.
evidence.summary: Official repository evidence for the principal manuscript named by Ellis: date, material, extent, work folios, appended map and verses, decoration, and current image availability.

evidence.citation: Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Arch. Selden. B. 10, Summary Catalogue 3356.
evidence.summary: Official repository evidence for the composite manuscript collated for Ellis: Hardyng's second version at fols. 1–199, its c. 1470–1480 date, later Lydgate part, decoration, former cover, provenance, and selected-image links.

open_questions.question: What narrower chronology and place or sequence of places can be assigned to Hardyng's Yorkist and Edwardian revision stages?
next_steps: Consult Peverley's detailed textual and biographical studies and subsequent second-version scholarship.
Distinguish dates of composition and revision from the later production dates of surviving manuscripts.
notes: The current record deliberately gives only England as a broad cultural origin and does not normalize an end year for an unfinished work.

open_questions.question: How do the CME containers and inherited 1812 headings map to a defensible canonical chapter sequence for the second version?
next_steps: Collate XML DIV2/@N, printed roman numerals, both 1543 prints, and the principal manuscript witnesses.
Retain encoding-specific section counts until repeated, missing, and mismatched labels are resolved.

open_questions.question: Which dialect characterization, if any, can responsibly be assigned to Hardyng's second-version work independently of particular manuscripts and printed normalization?
next_steps: Consult linguistic studies of the second-version witnesses and distinguish authorial language from scribal and print layers.
Do not infer a work-level dialect from the copied language of Harley 661, Arch. Selden. B. 10, Grafton's prints, or Ellis's reset.

notes: The catalog record describes historical claims, genealogy, territorial argument, and military planning as subjects of the work; it does not endorse the work's claims or proposed violence.
Edition, manuscript, facsimile, access, and rights facts are modeled in the linked lineage manifest rather than collapsed into work-level description.
All line and verse-group totals are reproducible encoding metrics from the pinned XML, not claims about Hardyng's autograph lineation or a complete critical text.
